title: Chapter 14: The First Progeny
content: Marcus Thorne had spent twelve years refusing to become what he hated.

Twelve years of vampires offering him the turning — Barons who wanted a blacksmith who could work with silver without burning his hands, Counts who admired his craftsmanship, even Dredd himself, once, when the Rot King had been drunk on tribute and feeling generous. Marcus had refused them all. He'd rather die human, he said, than live as one of the monsters who'd turned Greyhaven into a slaughterhouse.
And yet here he was, standing in Kael's war room at midnight, asking to be turned.
"I've got conditions," he said.
Kael looked up from the map table. "Name them."

"One: I don't kill innocents. Ever. If I'm becoming a vampire, I want a line I won't cross. A rule I can hold onto when the hunger gets bad." Marcus's voice was steady, but his hands — those massive blacksmith hands that had forged a thousand weapons — were trembling. "Two: you don't abandon me. I've seen what happens to fledglings without makers. They go feral. They get hunted. They die alone, and no one remembers their names."
"You'll never be alone."

"Three." Marcus met his eyes. "When this is over — when we've killed every Duke and Count and Prince who needs killing — you let me choose. Whether to keep living as a vampire or end it on my own terms."
The silence stretched. Outside, Greyhaven's bells tolled midnight — Kael's new bells, cast from the melted-down blood fountains and bone thrones of the old regime.
"That's a hard promise to make," Kael said.
"It's the only one that matters."

Kael thought about it. About the maker-progeny bond — the rule that said a maker bore responsibility for every crime their progeny committed. About the protection that flowed both ways — no vampire could harm Kael's progeny without triggering the Blood Curse on Kael himself. About the trust Marcus was offering, twelve years of hatred and resistance, surrendered in a single moment because he believed Kael was different.
"I promise," Kael said. "When the war is over, your life is your own."
Marcus nodded. He rolled up his sleeve. Extended his wrist.
"Do it."
The turning took two hours.

Marcus screamed for most of them. His human body fought the change with all the stubbornness he'd spent twelve years cultivating — bones reshaping, organs shutting down and restarting, every cell dying and being reborn as something other. Kael stayed with him through all of it, holding him down when the convulsions got too violent, talking to him through the worst of the pain.
"You're almost through," Kael said, as Marcus's spine arched and reformed. "Almost there. Stay with me."
"Can't—" Marcus's voice was a rasp, his throat raw from screaming. "Can't feel my—"
"You're feeling everything. That's the problem. Your nerves are rebuilding. Every sensation is new and wrong and overwhelming. But it passes. I promise you, it passes."

When it was over, Marcus stood. His eyes had changed — from grey to silver, faintly luminous. His muscles had thickened further, his posture shifted into something more predatory. But his face was still Marcus. Still human, somewhere underneath.
"How do you feel?" Kael asked.
"Like I could punch through a mountain." Marcus flexed his hands, watching the tendons move beneath his skin. "And hungry. Really, really hungry."
"There's blood in the kitchen. Human blood. Donated, not taken. From volunteers who understand what we're building."
"Donated?"

"We're building something different here. The humans who donate get paid, protection, food, a place in the new order. We don't take without consent." Kael met his eyes. "That's the rule. That's what makes us different from every vampire who came before."
Marcus was quiet for a long moment. Then he laughed — the same rough, surprised sound Kael had heard in his shop six weeks ago.
"You really are different," he said.
"I'm trying to be."
The first test came three days later.

Kael took Marcus to the surface. Dawn. The same pale grey light that had first touched Kael's face in the ruined church of Saint Michael's. Marcus stood at the tunnel entrance, staring at the sky with eyes that hadn't seen daylight in twelve years.
"I forgot what it looked like," he whispered.
"Are you ready?"
"I don't know. I've been underground so long, I don't remember what the sun feels like."
"It feels like warmth." Kael stepped into the light. The sun wrapped around him like a familiar cloak. "It feels like everything the old vampires told us we could never have."
Marcus took a breath. Stepped forward.
The sunlight touched his face.
Nothing happened. No smoke. No burning. No screaming. Just a newly-turned vampire, standing in full daylight, with tears running down his face.
"Twelve years," he said. "Twelve years underground, and I'm standing in the sun."
Kael put a hand on his shoulder. "This is what we're building. An army that doesn't fear the light. An army that can fight when every other vampire is helpless."
"The Polaris Guard," Marcus said.
"The North Star. The fixed point that never moves. The old vampires navigate by darkness. We navigate by something better."
"How many?"
"Eventually? Hundreds. For now, we start with a dozen. Hand-picked. Humans who've earned it. Humans who hate the old order as much as you do."
Marcus turned to face him. In the sunlight, his silver eyes looked almost human.
"You gave me my sky back," he said. "I'll follow you anywhere."

By the end of the first month, the Polaris Guard numbered twenty-three. Humans who'd survived the Rot King's tribute system, Silver Dawn defectors who'd heard rumors of the daylight Viscount, vampires who'd been stepped on by the Hierarchy their whole lives and wanted to step back. Marcus handled recruitment with the brutal honesty of someone who'd spent twelve years learning exactly what motivated desperate people.

"I don't promise them safety," he told Kael one night. "I promise them a chance. A chance to fight back. A chance to be part of something that matters. For most of them, that's worth more than safety ever was."

The training was brutal. Kael had been a scout before he'd been turned — he knew what military discipline looked like. He drilled his soldiers in formation tactics, in daylight assault patterns, in the precise ten-second window between a vampire waking from torpor and being able to defend themselves. He taught them to strike fast, to strike clean, to strike when the enemy was at their weakest.
He also taught them restraint. The Blood Curse was real. Every unsanctioned kill would mark him — not them, him — and he couldn't afford to become a target before the army was ready.
*Kill only in sanctioned combat*, he drilled into them. *Kill only when there's no other choice. Kill only when losing the battle means losing the war.*
And somewhere in the eastern territories, Count Orsi watched through his network of spies and began to understand that the three months he'd given Kael were not going to be enough.
